translations.translation:
Lou Xuan (Chengxian)
樓玄 (承先)
Lifespan Unlisted

byZL181
onOctober 15th, 2023

Lou Xuan, styledChengxian, was fromQi[蘄],Pei commandery[沛郡], During the reign ofSun Xiu, he was Imperial Censor Supervising Agriculture. AfterSun Haoassumed the throne, he,Wang Fan,Guo Chuo, andWan Yuwere all madeCavalier Regular Palace Attendants. He left to beGrand AdministratorofKuaiji, then entered to beMinister of Finance.
In the past, people who were in charge of the palace were selected from those intimate and close [with the emperor] to work, but[Wan] Yuexplained that for such intimate priviness and close knowledge, it was proper to use good people. Thus,[Sun] Haoreleased a decree, orderingdirecting officialsto seek loyal and pure officials and accept them in nominations.[Lou] Xuanwas employed to become Guardian of the Palace, Officer of the Palace, and Master of Hall Affairs.[Lou] Xuanwould go from being among theNine Ministersto wielding blades and being in attendance as a guard. He was upright as a person and he was a leading exemplar. He obeyed the laws and acted virtuously. His responses were candid and honest, and he opposed the will of[Sun] Haoon numerous occasions, thus receiving his resentment.
Later, there were people who slandered[Lou] XuanandHe Shao, saying that they, upon meeting by chance, stopped together, whispered to one another, and then laughed loudly as they were mocking government affairs. They thus were the subjects of an edict heavily rebuking them and sending them toGuang province[廣州]. TheDirector of the Eastern Tower,Hua He, petitioned:
“I, your minister, believe that the manner of governing a country is like running a household. The masters of fields and wildlands should all be virtuous and sincere, and it would also be appropriate for there to be one person who would compile clauses and make general rules, resulting in many a matter being organized. TheAnalectsstates, ‘One who does nothing and yet governs well can beShun! He was solemn and restrained in court, nothing more.’ This speaks of appointing and getting such people such that there may be comfort and respite. As of this moment, the lands within the Seas are not stabilized and the world has many problems. Such problems, regardless of severity, should all be referred, with any actions being undertaken by the throne, toiling your sagacious mind.Your Majestyhas set your intentions in extensively reading ancient texts and having extremely proficiency in the classics, adding onto your diligent heart and fondness for the moral path. When you follow your principles and devote your energy, you should be temperate and staid so as to exhibit divine thinking. You would become perfectly pure in one breath, and you would be at the same apex as Heaven. With each day, I consider that among the many officials that serve capably and are worthy enough to be entrusted with authority, none can fare better thanLou Xuan.[Lou] Xuanis pure, loyal, and serves the common good. He has a noble position at this age, the masses obey his virtuous conduct, and he never fights to be first. Regarding pure people, their minds are fair and their intentions are honest, and regarding loyal people, they think of the proper way and tread on them; such appears to be the personality of[Lou] Xuan, and he can maintain such [values]. I beseechYour Majestyto pardon the past faults of[Lou] Xuanto have him start anew. Promote him to a position akin to chancellor, then demand him for rendered deeds in the future. This will have him make offices and select people for them, and confer posts according to ability. That would be how solemn and restrainedShunwas, may it also be achieved in modern times!”
[Sun] Haohated the reputation and fame[Lou] Xuanhad, so he exiled[Lou] Xuanand his son,[Lou] Ju, and delivered them toJiaozhi[交阯] generalZhang Yi. He ordered them to fight to render achievements while secretly sending a separate edict to[Zhang] Yiwith orders to kill them.[Lou] Juwent toJiaozhi[交阯], but fell ill and died, so only[Lou] Xuanfollowed[Zhang] Yito suppress enemies, holding a sword wherever he treaded. Whenever he saw[Zhang] Yi, he always would pay respects.[Zhang] Yithus could not bear to kill him. When it happened that[Zhang] Yidied violently,[Lou] Xuancarried a coffin and put[Zhang] Yiinto it. Within the box, he saw the edict, so he killedhimself.A
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates:[Sun] Haosent generalZhang Yito pursue and give[Lou] Xuanpoison.[Zhang] Yi, as[Lou] Xuanwas a virtuous man, could not bear to announce the edict and give the poison.[Lou] Xuansecretly knew this and told[Zhang] Yi, “You should have told me earlier. Why would I be unhappy?” He took the poison and died.
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would comment on this.[Lou] Xuanwas pure and noble. He most certainly would not have changed his conduct regardless if he was in safety or danger. There was no reason for him to suddenly pay respects toZhang Yiand damage his moral integrity. Moreover, with disaster [the edict] being sent out, how would paying respects one hundred times exempt him from it? What theBiographies of the Region South of the Riversaid is superior in logic.
